_comment: Shri Hari Stotram (Jagajjālapālam) — Vishnu stotra in 8 verses with refrain 'bhajeham bhajeham', attributed to Swami Brahmananda. Canonical text sourced from Stotra Ratnavali (https://stotra.in/translation/en/sri-vishnu/shri-hari-stotram). Timings below are ESTIMATES; adjust start_time/end_time after watching cToCInaGzCw to match the actual audio. POST to /api/songs/verify with Authorization: Bearer <your-curator-JWT>.
title: Shri Hari Stotram (Jagajjālapālam)

lines.english_poetic: Om — I bow to Lord Nārāyaṇa.
lines.explanation: The ashtākṣara — the sacred eight-syllable Vishnu mantra — used as an invocation before reciting any Vaiṣṇava stotram.

lines.devanagari: जगज्जालपालं कचत्कण्ठमालं शरच्चन्द्रभालं महादैत्यकालं । नभोनीलकायं दुरावारमायं सुपद्मासहायं भजेऽहं भजेऽहम् ॥१॥
lines.iast: jagajjālapālam kacat-kaṇṭha-mālam śarac-candra-bhālam mahā-daitya-kālam | nabho-nīla-kāyam durāvāra-māyam supadmā-sahāyam bhajeham bhajeham ||1||
lines.english_literal: Protector of the world's web, shining garland on neck, autumn-moon forehead, death of great demons, sky-blue body, hard-to-overcome māyā, companion of beautiful Padmā — I worship, I worship.
lines.english_poetic: He who protects the woven fabric of the worlds, with a shining garland round his neck, his forehead radiant as the autumn moon, the death of mighty demons; whose body is dark as the open sky, whose māyā none can defeat, the consort of lotus-born Lakṣmī — I worship Him, I worship Him.
lines.explanation: Verse 1 establishes Hari as cosmic protector and demon-slayer, foreshadowing the themes of the entire stotram: he holds creation together while remaining transcendent. 'Padmā' is one of Lakṣmī's names.

lines.devanagari: सदाम्भोधिवासं गलत्पुष्पहासं जगत्सन्निवासं शतादित्यभासम् । गदाचक्रशस्त्रं लसत्पीतवस्त्रं हसच्चारुवक्त्रं भजेऽहं भजेऽहम् ॥२॥
lines.iast: sadāmbhodhi-vāsam galat-puṣpa-hāsam jagat-sannivāsam śatāditya-bhāsam | gadā-cakra-śastram lasat-pīta-vastram hasac-cāru-vaktram bhajeham bhajeham ||2||
lines.english_literal: Dwelling ever in the ocean, smile like a newly-opening flower, present throughout the world, radiance of a hundred suns; mace and discus as weapons, shining yellow garments, smiling beautiful face — I worship, I worship.
lines.english_poetic: He who reclines forever upon the milk ocean, whose smile blossoms like a fresh-opening flower, who dwells within every corner of the universe, who blazes with the radiance of a hundred suns; armed with mace and disc, robed in luminous yellow silk, his lovely face lit with a smile — I worship Him, I worship Him.
lines.explanation: Verse 2 paints Vishnu's iconography: reclining on Ananta-śeṣa in the cosmic ocean, holding the gadā (Kaumodakī) and cakra (Sudarśana), wearing the pītāmbara. The 'hundred suns' image is a classical Vedic measure of divine luminosity.

lines.devanagari: रमाकण्ठहारं श्रुतिव्रातसारं जलान्तर्विहारं धराभारहारम् । चिदानन्दरूपं मनोज्ञस्वरूपं धृतानेकरूपं भजेऽहं भजेऽहम् ॥३॥
lines.iast: ramā-kaṇṭha-hāram śruti-vrāta-sāram jalāntar-vihāram dharā-bhāra-hāram | cidānanda-rūpam manojña-svarūpam dhṛtāneka-rūpam bhajeham bhajeham ||3||
lines.english_literal: The necklace on Ramā's neck, essence of the Vedic collection, sporting within the waters, remover of earth's burden; whose form is consciousness-bliss, whose nature charms the mind, who has taken on many forms — I worship, I worship.
lines.english_poetic: He who hangs as a garland round the neck of Lakṣmī, the very essence of the gathered Vedas, who plays in the depths of the cosmic waters, who lifts the burden of the Earth; whose form is consciousness-bliss itself, whose true nature enchants the mind, who has assumed many incarnations — I worship Him, I worship Him.
lines.explanation: Verse 3 weaves four registers together: Vishnu as Lakṣmī's beloved, as the meaning of all Vedas, as the cosmic sleeper on Ananta, and as the lifter of the world's weight (echoing the avatāras of Varāha and Kūrma). 'Cit-ānanda' is the technical Vedāntic compound for the Self.

lines.devanagari: जराजन्महीनं परानन्दपीनं समाधानलीनं सदैवानवीनम् । जगज्जन्महेतुं सुरानीककेतुं त्रिलोकैकसेतुं भजेऽहं भजेऽहम् ॥४॥
lines.iast: jarā-janma-hīnam parā-nanda-pīnam samādhāna-līnam sadaivānavīnam | jagaj-janma-hetum surānīka-ketum trilokaika-setum bhajeham bhajeham ||4||
lines.english_literal: Free of old age and birth, full of supreme bliss, absorbed in samādhi, ever-new; cause of the world's birth, banner of the army of gods, sole bridge across the three worlds — I worship, I worship.
lines.english_poetic: He who is untouched by ageing or birth, brimming with the highest bliss, sunk in eternal samādhi, forever fresh; the very cause of the world's coming-into-being, the rallying banner of the army of gods, the one bridge spanning all three realms — I worship Him, I worship Him.
lines.explanation: The theologically densest verse: Vishnu is at once transcendent (beyond birth and decay, absorbed in samādhi) and immanent (cause of all creation, protector of devas, bridge between worlds). The Vedāntic paradox of the Absolute that is also active in the world is held in a single śloka.

lines.devanagari: कृताम्नायगानं खगाधीशयानं विमुक्तेर्निदानं हरारातिमानम् । स्वभक्तानुकूलं जगद्वृक्षमूलं निरस्तार्तशूलं भजेऽहं भजेऽहम् ॥५॥
lines.iast: kṛtāmnāya-gānam khagādhīśa-yānam vimukter-nidānam harārāti-mānam | svabhaktānukūlam jagad-vṛkṣa-mūlam nirastārta-śūlam bhajeham bhajeham ||5||
lines.english_literal: Sung by the Vedas, vehicle is the lord of birds, cause of liberation, humbler of Hara's enemies' pride; favourable to his own devotees, root of the world-tree, dispeller of suffering's sharp pain — I worship, I worship.
lines.english_poetic: He whose praises the Vedas sing, who rides upon Garuḍa lord of birds, the very cause of liberation, who breaks the pride of those who oppose Śiva; ever-favourable to his devotees, the deep root of this world-tree, who casts away the piercing pain of grief — I worship Him, I worship Him.
lines.explanation: Verse 5 highlights Vishnu as the source of mokṣa and as protector of dharma. 'Khagādhīśa' (Garuḍa) is his vāhana. The world-as-tree (jagad-vṛkṣa) is a recurring image — Vishnu is the root from which manifestation grows, echoing Gītā 15's aśvattha tree.

lines.devanagari: समस्तामरेशं द्विरेफाभकेशं जगद्बिम्बलेशं हृदाकाशदेशम् । सदा दिव्यदेहं विमुक्ताखिलेहं सुवैकुण्ठगेहं भजेऽहं भजेऽहम् ॥६॥
lines.iast: samastāmareśam dvirephābha-keśam jagad-bimba-leśam hṛdākāśa-deśam | sadā divya-deham vimuktākhileham suvaikuṇṭha-geham bhajeham bhajeham ||6||
lines.english_literal: Lord of all immortals, hair like a bee, the world is a tiny reflection in him, dwelling in the sky of the heart; ever divine of body, free from all worldly desires, his home the noble Vaikuṇṭha — I worship, I worship.
lines.english_poetic: He who is lord of all the immortals, his locks dark and lustrous as a bee, in whom the whole universe is but a small reflection, whose dwelling is the sky of the heart; whose body is forever divine, free from every worldly craving, whose home is fair Vaikuṇṭha — I worship Him, I worship Him.
lines.explanation: Verse 6 holds the cosmic and the intimate together: Vishnu is both lord of devas in Vaikuṇṭha and present in the 'sky of the heart' (hṛd-ākāśa) of the meditator. The image of the world as a 'small reflection' (bimba-leśa) within him inverts the usual cosmology — Vishnu is the original, the universe a passing image.

lines.devanagari: सुरालिबलिष्ठं त्रिलोकीवरिष्ठं गुरूणां गरिष्ठं स्वरूपैकनिष्ठम् । सदा युद्धधीरं महावीरवीरं भवाम्भोधितीरं भजेऽहं भजेऽहम् ॥७॥
lines.iast: surāli-baliṣṭham trilokī-variṣṭham gurūṇāṃ gariṣṭham svarūpaika-niṣṭham | sadā yuddha-dhīram mahāvīra-vīram bhavāmbhodhi-tīram bhajeham bhajeham ||7||
lines.english_literal: Mightier than the host of gods, foremost in the three worlds, weightiest among gurus, fixed in his own true Self; ever steady in battle, hero of heroes, the shore of the ocean of worldly existence — I worship, I worship.
lines.english_poetic: He who is mightier than the assembled host of gods, foremost in all three worlds, the gravest among gurus, firmly fixed in his own essential nature; ever calm in the heat of battle, the hero of heroes, the very shore beyond the ocean of saṃsāra — I worship Him, I worship Him.
lines.explanation: Verse 7 emphasises Vishnu's role as supreme warrior-teacher. 'Bhavāmbhodhi-tīram' — the shore of the ocean of becoming — is a classic image of mokṣa: worldly existence is a sea, the divine the further shore. 'Svarūpaika-niṣṭha' means he never wavers from his own true nature, a virtue the yogi imitates.

lines.devanagari: रमावामभागं तलानग्रनागं कृताधीनयागं गतारागरागम् । मुनीन्द्रैः सुगीतं सुरैः संपरीतं गुणौघैरतीतं भजेऽहं भजेऽहम् ॥८॥
lines.iast: ramā-vāma-bhāgam talānagra-nāgam kṛtādhīna-yāgam gatā-rāga-rāgam | munīndraiḥ sugītam suraiḥ samparītam guṇaughair-atītam bhajeham bhajeham ||8||
lines.english_literal: Ramā on his left side, resting on the serpent below, sacrifice subject to him, free of all attachment and passion; well-sung by chief sages, surrounded by gods, beyond the streams of guṇas — I worship, I worship.
lines.english_poetic: He who has Lakṣmī on his left, who reclines upon the cosmic serpent, to whom all sacrifice is rendered, free of every clinging and craving; whom the foremost sages sing in praise, whom the gods stand around in attendance, who is beyond the surging streams of the three guṇas — I worship Him, I worship Him.
lines.explanation: Verse 8 closes the stotram by gathering the deity's domestic, ritual, and metaphysical aspects: Lakṣmī beside him, Śeṣa-nāga beneath him, the yajña attaining its fruit through him, and his own transcendence of the three guṇas (sattva, rajas, tamas) — the very ground of Sāṃkhya-Vedānta cosmology.

lines.devanagari: इदं यस्तु नित्यं समाधाय चित्तं पठेदष्टकं कण्ठहारं मुरारेः । स विष्णोर्विशोकं ध्रुवं याति लोकं जराजन्मशोकं पुनर्विन्दते नो ॥
lines.iast: idaṃ yastu nityaṃ samādhāya cittaṃ paṭhed-aṣṭakaṃ kaṇṭha-hāraṃ murāreḥ | sa viṣṇor-viśokaṃ dhruvaṃ yāti lokaṃ jarā-janma-śokaṃ punar-vindate no ||
lines.english_literal: Whoever, having composed the mind, daily recites this octet — the necklace on Murāri's neck — he certainly goes to Vishnu's sorrowless world, and never again obtains the sorrow of old age and birth.
lines.english_poetic: Whoever, with a steady mind, recites these eight verses daily — this garland adorning Murāri's neck — surely goes to the sorrowless world of Vishnu, and is never again touched by the grief of birth and ageing.
lines.explanation: The phalashruti — the traditional 'fruit of recitation' verse that closes Sanskrit stotras. It names the eight verses as a 'kaṇṭha-hāra' (necklace) for Murāri (Vishnu, slayer of the demon Mura), and promises the meditative reciter passage to Vaikuṇṭha and freedom from the cycle of birth and death.
